note: Core verified claims for script anchoring, aligned to topic v002 angle (the too-perfect return chart as the smoking gun; the ignored whistleblower; the $65B myth-correction). R1: defendant deceased and pleaded guilty, so adjudicated facts can be stated plainly; still attribute trustee/OIG findings to their source and describe non-defendants neutrally. All dollar figures re-confirm against court/trustee record before script lock.

claims.normalized_claim: On March 12, 2009, Bernard L. Madoff pleaded guilty in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York to an 11-count criminal information (including securities fraud, investment adviser fraud, mail fraud, wire fraud, money laundering, false statements, perjury, and theft from an employee benefit plan) and was remanded into custody.
allowed_wording: he pleaded guilty to eleven federal felonies
he admitted it in open court
prohibited_wording: he was found guilty by a jury
claims.notes: He PLEADED guilty (no trial). Do not say 'convicted by a jury'.

claims.normalized_claim: On June 29, 2009, Madoff was sentenced to 150 years in federal prison, the statutory maximum, and later held at a Federal Correctional Complex in North Carolina.
allowed_wording: he was sentenced to 150 years
the maximum sentence

claims.normalized_claim: Madoff's investment advisory business operated as a Ponzi scheme: rather than investing client money, he deposited it in a bank account and paid requested 'returns' and withdrawals out of other investors' principal; investigators and the trustee found the advisory arm executed essentially no real securities trades for years and produced fabricated account statements and trade confirmations.
allowed_wording: he paid old investors with new investors' money
there were almost no real trades — the statements were fabricated
prohibited_wording: every part of his firm was fake (the separate market-making arm was a real business)
claims.notes: THE mechanism / spine. Distinguish the fraudulent advisory arm from Madoff's legitimate market-making/broker-dealer business.

claims.normalized_claim: The widely cited '$65 billion' figure is the total fabricated value reported across customer account statements (paper value including fictional profits), not the cash that went in; the trustee has estimated actual lost principal at approximately $17.5 billion.
allowed_wording: the famous $65 billion was mostly money that never really existed — fictional profits on paper
the real cash lost was about $17.5 billion
prohibited_wording: investors handed over $65 billion in cash
$65 billion was stolen and is gone
claims.notes: MYTH-CORRECTION payoff. Must be stated precisely so it doesn't create a new misunderstanding. $65B = fabricated statement value; ~$17.5B = principal.

claims.normalized_claim: Financial analyst Harry Markopolos repeatedly warned the SEC that Madoff's returns were mathematically impossible, submitting concerns to the SEC across roughly a decade (notably 2000, 2001, 2005, 2007 and 2008), including a detailed 2005 memo; the SEC did not stop the scheme before its 2008 collapse.
allowed_wording: a single analyst warned the SEC for years that the math was impossible
his repeated red-flag reports went unheeded
prohibited_wording: no one ever tried to warn anyone
claims.notes: HUMAN SPINE. The exact count/dates of submissions to be locked to the OIG report (SRC-0011) before script lock; the 2005 memo is often titled 'The World's Largest Hedge Fund Is a Fraud' — verify exact title from a primary copy before quoting it.

claims.normalized_claim: A central red flag was that Madoff's reported returns were unusually smooth and consistent — steady positive gains across years and across up and down markets — a pattern that is implausible for real investing and was the statistical tell the whistleblower relied on.
allowed_wording: returns too steady to be real
a line that almost never went down — and that was the warning sign
claims.notes: THE takeaway / thumbnail concept. Frame as the red flag in THIS case; explicitly NOT investment advice. Some interpretive content -> grade B, use measured wording.

claims.normalized_claim: The scheme unraveled during the 2008 financial crisis when investors sought to withdraw billions and Madoff could not meet redemptions; he confessed to his sons, who reported him to authorities, and he was arrested on December 11, 2008.
allowed_wording: the 2008 panic triggered a wave of withdrawals he couldn't cover
his sons reported him and he was arrested in December 2008
prohibited_wording: his sons were part of the fraud (they were not charged)
claims.notes: Do not imply the sons were complicit; neither was criminally charged. Describe neutrally.

claims.normalized_claim: Madoff's victims were not only individuals but also sophisticated institutions — banks, hedge funds and feeder funds, universities, and charities — some of which were forced to close; the breadth of expert victims underlines that a trusted name and smooth returns fooled professionals, not just amateurs.
allowed_wording: his victims included banks, charities and professional funds
even sophisticated investors were fooled
prohibited_wording: name specific living victims as foolish
claims.notes: Supports the 'smartest people fell for it' beat (angle element C). Keep neutral; do not single out or mock named victims; specific named institutions to be sourced before script lock.

claims.normalized_claim: Court-appointed SIPA trustee Irving Picard has recovered a large majority of the lost principal through settlements and clawbacks; by mid-2025 recoveries through the trustee totaled on the order of $14.7-15.3 billion, and combined with the DOJ Madoff Victim Fund many investors have recovered well over 90% of their actual losses.
allowed_wording: most of the real money was eventually clawed back
many victims recovered the large majority of what they put in
prohibited_wording: everyone got all their money back
claims.notes: Surprising, under-told beat (recovery). Confirm the latest exact totals from the trustee/DOJ before script lock; use ranges.

claims.normalized_claim: Bernard Madoff died in federal prison on April 14, 2021, at age 82, having served roughly 12 years of his sentence.
allowed_wording: he died in prison in 2021

claims.normalized_claim: The SEC's own Office of Inspector General later examined the agency's failure to uncover the scheme despite credible, specific complaints over many years, concluding the SEC had received but never substantiated the allegations through a competent examination.
allowed_wording: the SEC's own watchdog later found the agency missed repeated warnings
an internal report concluded the SEC never properly investigated the tips
claims.notes: Attribute to the OIG report; confirm exact report number/date/wording (SRC-0011) before stating specifics. Do not allege corruption — finding was failure/incompetence, not bribery.
